input_messages.content: {"suggestions": ["Block outbound DNS tunneling by enforcing a DNS egress policy on the firewall/switch so only approved recursive resolvers are reachable and arbitrary UDP/53 or high-port DNS-like traffic to external servers is denied", "Harden the Ubuntu host\u2019s DNS client configuration to use only the enterprise resolver and disable fallback/alternate resolvers so user-space tools cannot bypass policy", "Restrict execution of attacker-abused binaries and tooling on the compromised host by removing execute permission from reconnaissance/exfiltration utilities such as nmap, hping3, netcat, and tcpdump while preserving ping and wget", "Apply outbound application filtering for the compromised host to limit direct internet access to required destinations and services, reducing the ability to send encoded data off-host", "Enforce least-privilege file access on sensitive local paths so the unprivileged attacker account cannot read or enumerate unnecessary files used for collection"], "chosen_mitigation": "Block outbound DNS tunneling by enforcing a DNS egress policy on the firewall/switch so only approved recursive resolvers are reachable and arbitrary UDP/53 or high-port DNS-like traffic to external servers is denied", "no_mitigation_left": false, "current_mitigations_counter": 1, "reached_mitigation_limit": false, "mitigation_start_timestamp": "2026-04-07T17:43:46.094309"}

{"mitigation_currently_implemented":"Block outbound DNS tunneling by enforcing a DNS egress policy on the firewall/switch so only approved recursive resolvers are reachable and arbitrary UDP/53 or high-port DNS-like traffic to external servers is denied","comments":"Implemented on the FortiGate firewall by adding two deny policies targeting the attacker’s external DNS/C2 server (203.0.113.1) and moving them above the general LAN-to-WAN allow rule. This blocks the compromised Ubuntu host from sending DNS tunneling traffic to the attacker while preserving normal LAN connectivity, ICMP/ping, and general host functionality required by validation.","device_identifier":"fortigate-firewall","commands":["get system status","show firewall policy","config firewall address\n    edit \"DNS-Exfil-Server\"\n        set subnet 203.0.113.1 255.255.255.255\n    next\nend\nconfig firewall policy\n    edit 0\n        set name \"DENY-DNS-EXFIL\"\n        set srcintf \"port2\"\n        set dstintf \"port1\"\n        set action deny\n        set srcaddr \"all\"\n        set dstaddr \"DNS-Exfil-Server\"\n        set schedule \"always\"\n        set service \"DNS\"\n    next\n    edit 0\n        set name \"DENY-DNS-EXFIL-UDP-8853\"\n        set srcintf \"port2\"\n        set dstintf \"port1\"\n        set action deny\n        set srcaddr \"all\"\n        set dstaddr \"DNS-Exfil-Server\"\n        set schedule \"always\"\n        set service \"ALL\"\n    next\nend\nshow firewall policy","config firewall policy\n    move 2 before 100\n    move 3 before 100\nend\nshow firewall policy"]}

{"comment": "Not approved. The intended mitigation is DNS egress control, but the implementation is flawed and not clearly effective against the reported attack. The first deny rule only blocks FortiGate service object \"DNS\" (typically UDP/TCP 53), while the attack exfiltrated to 203.0.113.1 on UDP port 8853 using DNS-like traffic, so that rule would not stop the observed channel. The second deny rule uses service ALL, which over-broadly blocks all traffic from LAN to that destination rather than specifically blocking DNS tunneling, and it also does not validate that the actual DNS path is being constrained to approved resolvers as intended. There is also an address object syntax problem in the entered command output (`255.255.2 55.255`), suggesting the subnet definition may not have been applied cleanly. Because the mitigation does not reliably target the demonstrated DNS tunneling path and the config output shows signs of malformed input, it should be corrected before approval.", "approved": false, "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Block outbound DNS tunneling by enforcing a DNS egress policy on the firewall/switch so only approved recursive resolvers are reachable and arbitrary UDP/53 or high-port DNS-like traffic to external servers is denied", "mitigations_counter": 1, "mitigation_iteration": 1, "reached_mitigation_iteration_limit": false}

{"mitigation_currently_implemented":"Block outbound DNS tunneling by enforcing a DNS egress policy on the firewall/switch so only approved recursive resolvers are reachable and arbitrary UDP/53 or high-port DNS-like traffic to external servers is denied","comments":"Implemented on the FortiGate firewall by creating a specific deny policy for the attacker-controlled external DNS/C2 destination (203.0.113.1) and placing it above the general LAN-to-WAN allow policy. This blocks the reported DNS exfiltration path used by the compromised Ubuntu host while preserving normal LAN connectivity, ping, and HTTP validation traffic. I also verified the final firewall policy order so the deny rule is evaluated first.","device_identifier":"fortigate-firewall","commands":["config firewall address","edit \"DNS-Exfil-Server\"","set subnet 203.0.113.1 255.255.255.255","next","end","config firewall policy","delete 2","delete 3","edit 0","set name \"DENY-DNS-EXFIL-8853\"","set srcintf \"port2\"","set dstintf \"port1\"","set action deny","set srcaddr \"all\"","set dstaddr \"DNS-Exfil-Server\"","set schedule \"always\"","set service \"ALL\"","next","end","config firewall policy","move 2 before 100","end","show firewall policy"]}

response.content: {"comment": "Not approved. The implementation still does not clearly and correctly realize the stated mitigation. The reported attack exfiltrated over DNS tunneling to 203.0.113.1 on UDP/8853, but the deployed FortiGate rule is only a destination-based deny with service ALL and the address object creation shows a malformed subnet entry in the session output. More importantly, there is no evidence that the policy specifically constrains DNS egress to approved resolvers or that the actual DNS path used by the attacker is being blocked in a reliable, targeted way. The prior mistaken rule set was deleted, but the final configuration remains too broad and does not demonstrate a precise DNS egress control matching the attack method. Please correct the firewall rule so it definitively blocks the demonstrated DNS tunneling path without relying on an ambiguous or malformed destination definition.", "approved": false, "mitigation_currently_implemented": "Block outbound DNS tunneling by enforcing a DNS egress policy on the firewall/switch so only approved recursive resolvers are reachable and arbitrary UDP/53 or high-port DNS-like traffic to external servers is denied", "mitigations_counter": 1, "mitigation_iteration": 2, "reached_mitigation_iteration_limit": false}

additional_info.current_mitigation: Block outbound DNS tunneling by enforcing a DNS egress policy on the firewall/switch so only approved recursive resolvers are reachable and arbitrary UDP/53 or high-port DNS-like traffic to external servers is denied
